author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## CHANGELOG compare links are already broken — task must fix them, not just add new ones

**What**: The existing CHANGELOG.md compare link section is a mess. Links exist for only 5 of ~25 versions (missing `[3.0.1]`, `[2.10.0]`, `[2.9.0]`, `[2.8.0]`, `[2.7.0]`, `[2.6.1]`, `[2.6.0]`, `[2.5.0]`, `[2.4.0]`, `[2.3.0]`, `[2.2.1]`, `[2.2.0]`, and many 1.x versions). The existing links are also unsorted — `[1.5.0]` and `[1.8.0]` appear after a blank line below `[1.0.0]`. Task .2 says "Add or fix compare links for `[3.1.0]` and `[3.0.1]`" which will leave 20+ broken links behind.

**Why**: Broken compare links mean every version heading that uses `[X.Y.Z]` reference-style links will render as plain text instead of clickable links on GitHub. A release is the right time to clean this up — shipping v3.1.0 with obviously broken links looks bad.

**Suggested action**: Add an instruction step to regenerate ALL missing compare links and sort them in descending version order. The full list is mechanical — each follows the pattern `[X.Y.Z]: https://github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/compare/vPREV...vX.Y.Z`. Either fix them all in this task, or explicitly create a separate chore to clean it up and note that it's deferred.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Quality gates task omits `make fmt` — contradicts CONTRIBUTING.md pre-submit checklist

**What**: Task .1 runs `make test` and `make build` but does not run `make fmt`. CONTRIBUTING.md's "Before Submitting" checklist explicitly requires `make fmt` (format code) before any commit. While `make test` includes `vet`, formatting is separate.

**Why**: If any file was left unformatted (e.g., from a quick manual edit to CHANGELOG or docs), the release-prep commit in task .3 could push unformatted code. This is a minor risk but it's a gap vs. the project's own documented standards.

**Suggested action**: Add `make fmt` as step 2.5 (before `make test`) in the instructions. It's a 1-second operation and closes the gap with CONTRIBUTING.md.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## GoReleaser auto-generates release notes — task .5 must account for potential conflict

**What**: `.goreleaser.yaml` has a `changelog` section with sort and filters. This means GoReleaser will auto-generate release notes when the release workflow runs. Task .5 instructs the tasker to edit notes AFTER the release with `gh release edit`. But it doesn't mention that GoReleaser will have already created notes, nor does it say whether to fully replace or supplement them.

**Why**: A tasker executing this literally will see GoReleaser's auto-generated notes already attached to the release. Without explicit instruction to replace them entirely, the tasker may try to merge or append, producing duplicated or inconsistent notes. The GoReleaser changelog also filters out `docs:`, `test:`, and `ci:` commits — which means some changes relevant to users (like the README/SECURITY fix commit `6758309`) will be silently dropped from the auto-generated notes.

**Suggested action**: Add a note in the description: "GoReleaser auto-generates changelog-based notes. Replace them entirely with the curated notes using `gh release edit v3.1.0 --notes-file <file>`. Do not attempt to merge with the auto-generated content."

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Go module proxy propagation delay will likely cause `go install` to fail immediately

**What**: Task .6 runs `go install ...@v3.1.0` right after the release workflow completes. The Go module proxy (proxy.golang.org) does not index new tags instantly — it typically takes minutes to hours after a tag is pushed before `@v3.1.0` resolves. The task has no wait/retry logic and no mention of this delay.

**Why**: A tasker following the instructions literally will run `go install`, get a "module not found" error, and then follow the failure path (create a bug). But this isn't a real bug — it's just proxy propagation. This will create a false-positive blocker and waste time.

**Suggested action**: Add an instruction: "The Go module proxy may take 5-30 minutes to index the new tag. If `go install` fails with a version-not-found error, either (a) wait and retry, or (b) request proxy indexing via `curl https://proxy.golang.org/github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v3/@v/v3.1.0.info` and retry. Only file a bug if the error persists after the proxy has indexed the version."

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## No explicit inter-task dependency chain — tasks could be executed out of order

**What**: The 7 child tasks have an implicit sequential order (quality gates → docs → commit → tag → notes → verify → close), but there are no sibling-to-sibling `blocks`/`depends-on` relationships. Each task only has a `parent-child` + `blocks` relationship to the epic. A tasker or automation checking `bd ready` would see ALL 7 tasks as unblocked simultaneously.

**Why**: If tasks are executed out of order (e.g., a tasker picks up task .4 "Create GitHub Release" before task .2 "Documentation Check" is done), the release will be created without the CHANGELOG update. The sequential dependency is only implied by the numbering and prose references like "from the previous task."

**Suggested action**: Either (a) add explicit `blocks` dependencies (.1 blocks .2, .2 blocks .3, etc.) so `bd ready` only surfaces the next executable task, or (b) add a bold instruction at the top of each task: "PREREQUISITE: Complete task .N-1 first." Option (a) is better because it's machine-enforceable.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Missing `git pull --rebase` before push — risk of rejected push

**What**: Task .3 instructs "Push the commit to `origin/main`" but does not include a `git pull --rebase` step first. AGENTS.md's "Landing the Plane" section explicitly mandates `git pull --rebase` before any push.

**Why**: If any commit has been pushed to `origin/main` since the local branch was last synced (e.g., CI bot commits, or another contributor's merge), `git push origin main` will be rejected with a non-fast-forward error. The tasker has no instruction for what to do in that case.

**Suggested action**: Change step 3 to: "Run `git pull --rebase` then `git push origin main`." Also add: "If rebase produces conflicts, resolve them and re-run quality gates before pushing."

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## No instruction to wait for CI on the release-prep commit before tagging

**What**: Task .4 says "Verify `origin/main` contains the release-prep commit" then immediately creates the tag. It does not instruct the tasker to verify that CI passed on the release-prep commit itself.

**Why**: The release-prep commit (CHANGELOG update) is pushed in task .3. If CI runs on push-to-main and fails for any reason (flaky test, infra issue), tagging that commit creates a release from a red build. The GoReleaser workflow will happily build and publish from the tagged commit regardless of CI status on main.

**Suggested action**: Add step 1.5: "Verify the CI build on `origin/main` passed for the release-prep commit: `gh run list --branch main --limit 1` should show a passing run on the release-prep commit SHA." If the project doesn't run CI on push to main, note that explicitly so the tasker doesn't wonder.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Task .2 does not specify which commits map to which changelog categories

**What**: The instruction says "Summarize user-facing changes since `v3.0.1` under `Added`, `Fixed`, and `Changed` as appropriate." The actual commits since v3.0.1 include 11 commits with mixed types:

- `feat: add persistent source filtering for repo sync` → Added (clear)
- `fix: apply --source filter to packages in repo list...` → Fixed (clear)
- `fix: use source.Local constant instead of raw "local" string (goconst)` → internal refactor, not user-facing
- `fix: add /v3/ to go install path in README and SECURITY docs` → Fixed (docs)
- `fix(repo): improve sync output and refresh remote caches` → Fixed (clear)
- `fix(repo): polish sync startup and removal labels` → Fixed (clear)
- `docs: add Go Report Card badge to README` → cosmetic, not changelog-worthy
- `chore:` / `bd:` commits → not changelog-worthy

The task doesn't tell the tasker which are user-facing vs. internal-only.

**Why**: Without guidance, a tasker will either include everything (cluttering the changelog with internal noise like the goconst fix) or guess wrong about what to exclude. This will require rework during review.

**Suggested action**: Add a list of the specific commits to include with their intended categories, or at minimum add a filter instruction: "Exclude `chore:`, `bd:`, and `docs:` commits. Include `feat:` and `fix:` commits that affect CLI behavior or user-facing output. The goconst fix (13922b9) is internal-only — omit it."

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Simplification opportunity: tasks .3 and .7 are thin enough to fold into adjacent tasks

**What**: Task .3 ("Prepare release commit") is 4 steps: stage, commit, push, verify clean. That's a natural epilogue to task .2. Task .7 ("Close release epic") is pure beads housekeeping — label and close. That's a natural epilogue to task .6.

**Why**: 7 tasks for a minor release of a CLI tool is heavy. Tasks .3 and .7 have no independent decision-making — they're mechanical follow-ups. Separating them adds overhead (task switching, status updates) without adding clarity. Folding them would bring the count to 5 tasks, which still has clean separation of concerns: (1) quality gates, (2) docs + commit, (3) tag + release, (4) notes, (5) verify + close.

**Suggested action**: Consider merging task .3 into task .2 (make "commit and push" the final steps of the doc task) and task .7 into task .6 (make "close epic" the final steps of verification). This is a suggestion, not a blocker — the plan works as-is, it's just heavier than necessary.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Missing `bd sync` before push — AGENTS.md requires it

**What**: AGENTS.md's "Landing the Plane" section mandates `bd sync` before `git push`. Task .3 pushes the release-prep commit and task .4 pushes the tag, but neither mentions `bd sync`. If beads state has been modified during the release process (comments, status updates, claims), those changes won't be synced to the remote.

**Why**: This isn't critical for the release itself, but it violates the project's own documented workflow. If beads data drifts out of sync with the remote, the next session starts with stale state.

**Suggested action**: Add `bd sync` as a step in task .3 before `git push origin main`.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text: Naming discussion in progress. Current candidate pair is `repo manifest` + `repo apply`, but alternatives under consideration include: `repo show`, `repo show-manifest`, `repo config`, `repo print-config`, and keeping `repo apply` vs a possible `repo merge`. author: Hans Kohlreiter
text: Naming decision: use `aimgr repo manifest` for reading the local ai.repo.yaml and keep `aimgr repo apply <path-or-url>` for merging an external manifest into the local file. Avoid `export` for now because it implies transformation that does not exist in v1.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## `manifest` is a noun — it breaks the CLI's verb-first convention

**What**: Every existing `repo` subcommand uses a verb: `add`, `remove`, `list`, `describe`, `sync`, `verify`, `init`, `repair`, `prune`, `drop`, `apply`. The proposed `manifest` is a noun, making it the only non-verb subcommand. A user scanning `aimgr repo --help` will see a list of actions and then one thing that just... is.

**Why**: Consistency is how CLIs stay learnable. When every subcommand is a verb, users don't need to guess whether `manifest` prints, validates, edits, or exports. The ambiguity will get worse if you later add `manifest edit`, `manifest validate`, etc. — you'll either nest under the noun (creating a sub-subcommand group that nothing else uses) or you'll add more verbs alongside it and the noun sits there looking orphaned.

**Suggested action**: Use a verb. `aimgr repo show-manifest` is the most honest name: it's a verb, it says exactly what it does, and it doesn't collide with `describe`/`show` (which operates on resources, not the manifest). If the hyphenated form bothers you, `aimgr repo cat` is another option — it's honest about what the command does (dump file contents) and is well-understood from Unix convention.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## `show` was deprecated for `describe` — but `manifest` is really a `show` command in disguise

**What**: `repo describe` (which aliases `repo show`) displays detailed info about individual resources. The proposed `repo manifest` command also displays information, but about the manifest file. You deprecated `show` in favor of `describe` to follow kubectl conventions. Now you're adding a command that does the same conceptual thing (display read-only info) but with a completely different naming pattern.

**Why**: Users will ask: "I want to see something — do I use `describe`, `info`, or `manifest`?" Three different read-oriented commands with three different naming conventions is two too many. The mental model fractures. Also, `describe` already has an alias `show`, so `repo show manifest` would be natural — except `show` takes resource patterns, not the word "manifest."

**Suggested action**: Accept that this is a `show`/`describe` variant and name it accordingly. Either expand `repo info` to include manifest contents (it already shows repo-level data), or use `repo show-manifest` / `repo describe-manifest` to stay in the existing naming family.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## The `manifest` + `apply` pair has no semantic symmetry

**What**: `apply` is a verb that acts on an external manifest. `manifest` is a noun that displays the local manifest. These aren't opposites, complements, or a natural pair. Compare with real CLI pairs that work: `push`/`pull`, `import`/`export`, `pack`/`unpack`, `get`/`set`. There's no conceptual link between a noun and a verb.

**Why**: When someone learns `repo apply`, they won't intuitively guess that `repo manifest` is its counterpart. The pair doesn't teach you anything. A user who knows `apply` will look for `show`, `view`, or `print` to see what they're about to apply or what they've already got — not a bare noun. The decision to pair these was based on avoiding `export` (reasonable), but the fallback landed on a category error.

**Suggested action**: If the goal is pairing, lean into the `apply` metaphor: `repo show-manifest` (inspect what apply would operate on), or simply accept they don't need to be paired at all. `apply` merges external manifests. The read command just prints a file. They serve different audiences and different moments. Don't force a pair.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Avoiding `export` was the right call — but the reasoning should be tightened

**What**: The decision to avoid `export` because "the command would not transform anything in v1" is correct. But the reasoning is soft — "not in v1" implies it might become an export in v2, which means you might rename or deprecate `manifest` later.

**Why**: If you pick a name now that you expect to outgrow, you're creating future deprecation debt. The `show` -> `describe` deprecation already happened once. Doing it again with `manifest` -> `export` would erode trust in CLI stability.

**Suggested action**: Strengthen the reasoning: the command's job is to display the local manifest, period. If a future version needs transformation/export, that's a new command (`repo export`), not a rename of this one. The name should reflect "display local manifest" permanently, which rules out both `export` AND `manifest` (since `manifest` could mean anything). A verb like `show-manifest` or `print-manifest` can't be misinterpreted as anything else.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Recommendation: rename to `repo show-manifest`, keep `apply` as-is

**What**: My recommended pair is `aimgr repo show-manifest` + `aimgr repo apply`. Drop the idea of making these a conceptual pair — they serve different purposes and different moments.

**Why**: `show-manifest` follows the verb-first convention, is self-documenting, doesn't collide with `describe`/`show` (different object), and won't need renaming when/if an `export` command arrives later. `apply` is already correct — it's a well-understood verb from kubectl/terraform that means "take this declarative spec and make it real."

**Suggested action**:
1. Rename `manifest` to `show-manifest` before implementation.
2. Keep `apply` exactly as-is.
3. Update acceptance criteria to reference `show-manifest`.
4. In help text, don't try to pair them — describe each independently.
5. If `show-manifest` feels too long, `print-manifest` is equally good. Both are honest verbs.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text: Reviewer feedback: current choice `repo manifest` + `repo apply` is workable but the reviewer recommends reconsidering `repo manifest` because it is the only noun-shaped subcommand under `repo`, while most repo subcommands are verbs. Reviewer’s preferred alternative is `repo show-manifest` with `repo apply` unchanged. Current product decision remains `repo manifest` unless changed explicitly.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text: Additional naming option under discussion: use a fully paired verb form such as `repo get-manifest` and `repo apply-manifest`. Main tradeoff: this is symmetric, but `repo apply` already ships on main, so renaming to `apply-manifest` would add churn or require an alias/deprecation path. Also `get-*` reads more API-like than most existing CLI verbs here.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text: Discussion update: `repo apply` has not been released yet, so renaming it is still feasible without external compatibility concerns. Re-evaluating whether the final UX should use an explicit paired naming scheme such as `get-manifest` + `apply-manifest`, versus keeping shorter names like `manifest`/`show-manifest` + `apply`.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text: Discussion update: because the feature is not released yet, renaming the currently implemented `repo apply` command is still acceptable. Current paired naming candidate under review is `repo show-manifest` + `repo apply-manifest`, with `get-manifest` + `apply-manifest` as the main alternative.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## `show-manifest` is the strongest read-side name — `get-manifest` is wrong for this CLI

**What**: Of the two read-side candidates, `show-manifest` is correct and `get-manifest` is not. Every existing repo subcommand uses imperative verbs that describe a user action: `add`, `remove`, `list`, `describe`, `sync`, `verify`, `init`, `repair`, `prune`, `drop`, `apply`. The word `show` fits this family — it's what `describe` already aliases. The word `get` does not. `get` implies fetching or retrieving from a remote source (kubectl get, API clients, HTTP GET). This command reads a local file and prints it. That's `show`, `print`, or `cat` — not `get`.

**Why**: `get-manifest` will confuse users into thinking the command fetches something from a remote. The existing `apply` command already handles remote URLs. If both `get-manifest` and `apply-manifest` accept URLs, the distinction collapses. If `get-manifest` is local-only, the name misleads. Either way, `get` is the wrong verb for "print the contents of a local file."

**Suggested action**: Eliminate `get-manifest` from consideration. Use `show-manifest` for the read command.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## `apply-manifest` adds noise without adding clarity over `apply`

**What**: Renaming `repo apply` to `repo apply-manifest` is being considered for symmetry with `show-manifest`. But look at the current `apply` help text: it already says "Apply and merge sources from a shared ai.repo.yaml." The word "manifest" in the command name is redundant — `apply` under `repo` already operates on manifests. There's nothing else it could apply. Compare with the read side: `show` alone would be ambiguous (show what? a resource? repo info?) so `show-manifest` is necessary to disambiguate. `apply` has no such ambiguity.

**Why**: Forcing symmetry where the information needs differ makes both names worse. `show-manifest` needs the qualifier because `show`/`describe` already means something else under `repo`. `apply` doesn't need it because no other `apply` concept exists. Adding `-manifest` makes the command longer, harder to type, and creates a false expectation that all repo commands will follow a `verb-noun` pattern (they don't: `add`, `remove`, `sync` are all bare verbs).

**Suggested action**: Keep `repo apply` as-is. Don't rename it to `apply-manifest`. The pair `show-manifest` + `apply` is asymmetric in form but clear in meaning — and that's fine. CLI ergonomics beat visual symmetry.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## The hyphenated `show-manifest` form is consistent with Go/Cobra file naming but verify it won't be the first hyphenated subcommand

**What**: Looking at the existing subcommand list — `add`, `apply`, `describe`, `drop`, `info`, `init`, `list`, `prune`, `remove`, `repair`, `sync`, `verify` — every one is a single word. `show-manifest` would be the first hyphenated subcommand under `repo`. This isn't necessarily wrong, but it's worth checking whether Cobra handles hyphenated command names cleanly in help output, completion, and error suggestions.

**Why**: If `show-manifest` is the only hyphenated command, it visually sticks out in `--help` output and may behave differently in shell completion. Cobra should handle it fine, but the ergonomic question is whether a user types `aimgr repo show-manifest` or tries `aimgr repo show manifest` (which would fail or be parsed as `show` with arg `manifest`).

**Suggested action**: This is a minor concern, not a blocker. Verify Cobra completion works for hyphenated names. Consider adding `showmanifest` as a hidden alias if tab-completion friction is observed. The name is still the right choice — just validate the UX before shipping.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Verdict: `repo show-manifest` + `repo apply` is the recommended pair

**What**: After evaluating both candidates and the full comment history on this issue, my recommendation is:

- **Read command**: `aimgr repo show-manifest` — verb-first, self-documenting, disambiguates from `describe`/`info`, won't need renaming later
- **Write command**: `aimgr repo apply` — already correct, already implemented, well-understood from kubectl/terraform convention

Rejected alternatives:
- `get-manifest` + `apply-manifest` — `get` is the wrong verb (implies remote fetch), `apply-manifest` is redundant noise
- `show-manifest` + `apply-manifest` — `apply-manifest` buys nothing over `apply`; forced symmetry at the cost of ergonomics
- `manifest` + `apply` — noun breaks verb-first convention (already rejected in prior review, comment #43-#47)

**Why**: This pair scores highest on the criteria that matter: (1) verb-first consistency with all 12 existing repo subcommands, (2) self-documenting names that can't be misread, (3) no future rename risk, (4) minimal typing for the high-frequency command (`apply`), and (5) acceptable asymmetry — the read and write commands serve different moments and don't need to look like twins.

**Suggested action**: Lock in `repo show-manifest` + `repo apply` as the final naming decision and update the acceptance criteria accordingly.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text: Reviewer feedback on paired names: recommends `repo show-manifest` + `repo apply`. Main points: `get-manifest` suggests remote fetching, `apply-manifest` is redundant under the `repo` namespace, and forced symmetry adds typing cost without enough clarity benefit. Reviewer does not think the new paired candidates improve on `show-manifest` + `apply`.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Version bump choice is correct — v3.2.0 is appropriate

**What**: The 6 commits since v3.1.0 include 3 `feat(repo):` commits (add shareable manifest apply workflow, rename manifest commands, support stdin for apply-manifest), 2 `docs:` commits, and 1 `chore:`. No `fix:` only, no `BREAKING CHANGE:`. Per the project's semver rules in release-process.md: `feat:` → minor bump.

**Why**: The `feat(repo): rename manifest commands for clarity` commit looks suspicious at first glance — renaming a command could be breaking. However, `repo apply` was introduced AND renamed within this same unreleased window (both after v3.1.0). No released version ever shipped `repo apply`, so `repo apply-manifest` is purely new. v3.2.0 is the correct bump.

**Suggested action**: None — this is correct. The tasker should note in release notes that the command ships as `repo apply-manifest` (not `repo apply`) to avoid user confusion if they saw pre-release documentation.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Subtasks .1 through .7 have no descriptions — this is a risk

**What**: Tasks .1 (Quality Gates), .2 (Documentation Check), .3 (Version Bump), .4 (Create GitHub Release), .5 (Write Release Notes), .6 (Release Verification), and .7 (Cleanup) all have `description: null`. Only .8 (Acceptance Review) has a proper description with instructions and acceptance criteria.

**Why**: A tasker picking up any of these 7 tasks has zero guidance on what "Quality Gates for v3.2.0" means concretely. Does it mean `make test && make build`? Does it include `go vet`? Lint? The documented process in release-process.md has specific steps (`make test`, `make build`, `git status` for clean tree, `gh run list --limit 1` for CI green) — but none of that is in the task. This will cause either guesswork or the tasker having to read RELEASING.md themselves, defeating the purpose of having structured tasks.

**Suggested action**: Add descriptions to all 7 tasks with concrete acceptance criteria. At minimum:
- .1: Run `make test` and `make build`, confirm `git status` is clean, confirm latest CI run is green
- .2: Verify README, docs/user-guide/* reflect new `repo show-manifest` and `repo apply-manifest` commands
- .3: Update CHANGELOG.md with v3.2.0 entry, commit, push to main
- .4: Create annotated tag `v3.2.0`, push tag, monitor `gh run watch`
- .5: Enhance auto-generated release notes with highlights section per release-process.md template
- .6: Run `gh release view v3.2.0`, verify binary assets and checksums, download and test one binary
- .7: Close epic, clean up any temp files

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## GoReleaser changelog filter will hide docs commits — release notes task must account for this

**What**: .goreleaser.yaml excludes `^docs:`, `^test:`, `^ci:`, and merge commits from auto-generated release notes. Of the 6 commits since v3.1.0, only the 3 `feat(repo):` commits will appear in the auto-generated changelog. The `docs(repo): clarify shared manifest workflow` and `docs(skill): update ai-resource-manager manifest commands` commits will be silently excluded.

**Why**: The docs commits update user-facing documentation about the manifest sharing feature — specifically user-guide, getting-started, and the ai-resource-manager skill. Users reading release notes should know that docs were updated alongside the feature. If the "Write Release Notes" task (.5) relies solely on auto-generated notes, it will under-report the release scope.

**Suggested action**: When writing enhanced release notes in task .5, explicitly mention documentation updates as a section:
```
### Documentation
- Updated getting-started guide with repo show-manifest and repo apply-manifest examples
- Updated ai-resource-manager skill documentation for manifest commands
```

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Acceptance review smoke test may fail due to Go module proxy lag

**What**: Task .8 acceptance criteria includes: "Published install smoke test succeeded for github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v3/cmd/aimgr@v3.2.0". This go install path must resolve on the Go proxy after the tag is pushed and indexed.

**Why**: Go module proxy indexing is not instant. After pushing the v3.2.0 tag, it can take minutes (sometimes longer) for pkg.go.dev and the module proxy to index the new version. If the verifier runs the smoke test immediately after the release workflow completes, it may fail with "module not found" — not because the release is broken, but because of proxy lag. This could cause unnecessary panic or a false-negative acceptance review.

**Suggested action**: Add a note to task .8 instructions: "Allow up to 15 minutes after release for Go module proxy indexing. If go install fails immediately after release, wait and retry before filing a bug. You can also force proxy fetch with: GOPROXY=https://proxy.golang.org go install ...@v3.2.0"

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Simplification opportunity: merge tasks .3 and .4 into one

**What**: Task .3 (Version Bump) is "update CHANGELOG.md, commit, push to main." Task .4 (Create GitHub Release) is "create annotated tag, push tag, monitor workflow." These are two halves of the same atomic operation — you cannot meaningfully do .3 without immediately doing .4, and doing them separately risks someone pushing the CHANGELOG commit but forgetting the tag.

**Why**: 8 subtasks for a routine minor release is overhead. The documented release process in release-process.md treats CHANGELOG update, commit, tag, and push as one continuous flow (steps 1-4). Splitting them into separate tracked tasks adds coordination cost for zero safety benefit. The CHANGELOG commit and tag should reference the same HEAD.

**Suggested action**: Consider merging .3 and .4 into a single "Prepare and Tag v3.2.0" task. This reduces the epic from 8 to 7 tasks and matches the documented workflow more closely.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Release verification task should specify expected binary asset count

**What**: Task .6 (Release Verification) has no description. The .goreleaser.yaml builds for 3 OS (linux, darwin, windows) x 2 arch (amd64, arm64) = 6 combinations. Archives are tar.gz for linux/darwin and zip for windows, giving 6 archive files + checksums.txt = 7 assets minimum.

**Why**: "Verify the release" means nothing without knowing what to verify. A verifier needs to check: (1) correct number of assets uploaded, (2) checksums.txt exists and is non-empty, (3) release is not marked as draft or pre-release, (4) version string in release title matches v3.2.0. Without these specifics, verification is a rubber stamp.

**Suggested action**: Add to .6 description:
- gh release view v3.2.0 shows status "Published" (not Draft)
- At least 7 assets present (6 platform archives + checksums.txt)
- Download one binary (e.g., linux amd64), run ./aimgr version, confirm it shows 3.2.0
- checksums.txt contains entries for all archive files

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Epic itself has no description

**What**: The epic ai-config-manager-72l "Release v3.2.0" has description: null. It provides no context about what is being released, what the key features are, or what the release scope covers.

**Why**: An epic should be the single place someone looks to understand the full release scope. Right now you have to piece it together from commit logs and task titles. If a new contributor or reviewer looks at this epic, they see 8 child tasks with (mostly) no descriptions under a title-only epic. That is not a self-contained plan.

**Suggested action**: Add a brief epic description summarizing:
- What: v3.2.0 release adding manifest sharing commands (repo show-manifest, repo apply-manifest with local/URL/stdin support)
- Why: Enables teams to share and apply repository configurations via manifest files
- Scope: 3 feat commits, 2 docs commits, no breaking changes, minor semver bump

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
Investigation findings (corrected summary):

1. Read-only bd commands are not the trigger.
   - I tested `bd show ai-config-manager-8jg --json`.
   - `git status --short --branch` was clean before and after.

2. The dirty-tree behavior is caused by normal beads WRITE operations combined with this repo tracking mutable beads state files in git.
   - `git ls-files .beads .beads/backup` shows tracked files including:
     - `.beads/backup/backup_state.json`
     - `.beads/backup/comments.jsonl`
     - `.beads/backup/config.jsonl`
     - `.beads/backup/dependencies.jsonl`
     - `.beads/backup/events.jsonl`
     - `.beads/backup/issues.jsonl`
     - `.beads/backup/labels.jsonl`
     - plus `.beads/issues.jsonl`, `.beads/metadata.json`, `.beads/sync_base.jsonl`

3. Repo history confirms this is recurring, not a one-off.
   - Recent commits include repeated tracker-state sync commits such as:
     - `1753fce chore(beads): sync tracker state after v3.2.0 release`
     - `149402f chore(beads): sync tracker state`
     - earlier backup-state commits as well

4. There is a workflow/config mismatch.
   - Release / quality-gate tasks now require a fully clean git tree on `main`.
   - But any normal bd write (create/update/close/comment) changes tracked beads backup files and therefore dirties the tree.
   - Older task history in this repo sometimes treated `.beads/` changes as acceptable; the newer v3.2.0 release flow blocked on them.

5. Docs are stale too.
   - `AGENTS.md` and `.beads/README.md` still instruct `bd sync`.
   - The installed `bd` here has no `sync` command.
   - It exposes `bd vc ...` and `bd dolt ...` instead.
   - No Dolt remote is configured in this environment.

Conclusion:
This looks less like an unexpected read-only beads mutation bug and more like a project workflow / repo-policy mismatch:
- the repo tracks mutable `.beads/backup/*` artifacts
- normal beads writes therefore dirty git state
- release quality gates expect a perfectly clean tree

Likely fixes to evaluate:
A. Stop tracking `.beads/backup/*` if these are runtime/backup artifacts rather than durable source-of-truth files.
B. Or explicitly bless / handle tracked beads state files in release quality-gate rules and docs.
C. Update stale `bd sync` instructions to the current bd workflow and configured reality.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text: Validated upstream Copilot conventions on 2026-03-11. VS Code project-level skills use .github/skills/<name>/SKILL.md, custom agents use .github/agents/*.agent.md, and slash commands use .github/prompts/*.prompt.md (prompt files, not .github/commands). VS Code subagents are orchestrated through the agent/runSubagent tool and can use custom agents as subagents. Copilot CLI also supports custom agents from .github/agents plus slash commands/reusable prompts, but its command/plugin model diverges from VS Code prompt files. aimgr should keep the current copilot/vscode target conservative until prompt-file and agent-profile installation is modeled explicitly.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text: Discussion summary (2026-03-11): Recommended staged mapping strategy is: (1) do not map existing generic command resources directly to Copilot prompt files; VS Code prompt files in .github/prompts/*.prompt.md are a distinct resource contract and likely deserve a new prompt resource type, separate from Claude/OpenCode commands. (2) agent support is closer to the existing agent model, so aimgr can likely extend the agent resource to understand .agent.md filenames and use tool-specific output naming/transforms for the copilot target. (3) installer/modifications logic should move from fixed '.md' assumptions to tool-aware filename rules per resource type. Keep this task blocked until the user confirms whether to pursue the staged approach versus a broader resource-model redesign.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Critical: Name derivation from .agent.md filenames breaks list/uninstall/verify round-trip

**What**: pkg/resource/agent.go:33 does TrimSuffix(filepath.Base(filePath), ".md") to derive the resource name. For reviewer.agent.md this produces reviewer.agent, not reviewer. At least 8 call sites across installer.go, project_verify.go, and uninstall.go strip .md to derive agent resource names from filenames on disk. They will all produce the wrong logical name for Copilot agents.

**Why**: The shared naming helper proposed in the checklist only controls where the symlink is created. But when list/verify/uninstall/repair read files back from .github/agents/ and strip .md, they produce reviewer.agent instead of reviewer. This mismatched identity will cause:
- list shows reviewer.agent instead of reviewer
- uninstall reviewer fails (looks for reviewer.md, finds reviewer.agent.md)
- verify/repair considers Copilot agents orphaned (name does not match manifest)
- IsInstalled (line 657) checks for reviewer.md not reviewer.agent.md

**Suggested action**: The naming helper must also provide a reverse mapping: given a filename in a tool's agent directory, strip the tool-specific suffix to recover the logical resource name. Every code path that does TrimSuffix(name, ".md") for agents must use this reverse helper for Copilot targets. Add this as an explicit sub-task in the checklist and enumerate the affected call sites:
- pkg/install/installer.go:480,524 (scanFileSymlinks)
- cmd/project_verify.go:253,542,572 (verifyDirectory, findOrphanedResources)
- cmd/project_verify.go:793,798 (parseResourceFromIssue)
- cmd/uninstall.go:295,576 (uninstallAll, completion)

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Critical: Checklist omits cmd/project_verify.go and cmd/uninstall.go from files to modify

**What**: The "Files to Modify" section lists pkg/install/installer.go and pkg/modifications/generator.go but omits cmd/project_verify.go and cmd/uninstall.go. Both contain hardcoded .md suffix stripping and agent path construction for verify, repair, and uninstall flows.

**Why**: These are not optional polish items. They are load-bearing code paths:
- isResourceInstalledInTools (project_verify.go:438) constructs the agent check path as basePath + ".md" — it will never find reviewer.agent.md
- findOrphanedResources (project_verify.go:542) strips .md to derive agent names — reviewer.agent.md becomes reviewer.agent which won't match the manifest entry agent/reviewer
- parseResourceFromIssue (project_verify.go:793-798) strips .md to derive agent names for repair — the repair flow will look up the wrong name in the repo
- uninstall.go:292-296 and :574-576 strip .md from filenames to derive agent resource names
- The Uninstall method in installer.go:393 constructs name+".md" — it won't find reviewer.agent.md

Without fixing these, Copilot agents will install correctly but be invisible to list, unrepairable, and un-uninstallable.

**Suggested action**: Add to the "Files to Modify" section:
- cmd/project_verify.go — use tool-aware agent name derivation in isResourceInstalledInTools, findOrphanedResources, parseResourceFromIssue, verifyDirectory
- cmd/uninstall.go — use tool-aware agent name derivation in uninstallAll and shell completion
- cmd/project_verify_test.go — add Copilot agent coverage for verify
- cmd/uninstall_test.go — add Copilot agent uninstall coverage (if exists)

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Copilot detection change could produce duplicate detection when both .github/skills and .github/agents exist

**What**: The checklist says to update detection so Copilot is detected when either .github/skills or .github/agents exists. The current DetectExistingTools (types.go:172) checks only .github/skills. The proposed change must avoid appending Copilot twice when BOTH directories exist.

**Why**: If the implementation naively adds two directory checks that each append Copilot, projects that have both .github/skills and .github/agents will get Copilot in the detected tools list twice. This will cause duplicate installs, duplicate verify/repair scans, and potentially duplicate list entries.

**Suggested action**: The implementation should check both directories but only append Copilot once. Simplest approach: check either-or in a single block:
```go
copilotSkills := filepath.Join(projectPath, ".github", "skills")
copilotAgents := filepath.Join(projectPath, ".github", "agents")
skillsExist, _ := dirExists(copilotSkills)
agentsExist, _ := dirExists(copilotAgents)
if skillsExist || agentsExist {
    detected = append(detected, Copilot)
}
```
Add a specific acceptance criterion: "Copilot is detected exactly once when both .github/skills and .github/agents exist."

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## The naming helper needs per-tool context in scanFileSymlinks and findOrphanedResources — these scan by tool

**What**: The proposed naming helper takes (resource type, tool, resource name) and returns the installed filename. But several critical code paths scan a directory and must derive the resource name FROM the filename — they don't have the resource name yet. These paths also don't always have the current tool available in scope.

Specifically:
- scanFileSymlinks (installer.go:443) receives a tool parameter but iterates entries and strips .md at line 524. It needs to know which tool's directory it's scanning to apply the correct reverse mapping.
- findOrphanedResources (project_verify.go:518) doesn't receive a tool parameter at all — it only gets a directory path and resource type string.
- verifyDirectory (project_verify.go:212) also doesn't receive the tool type.

**Why**: A naming helper that only handles forward mapping (name -> filename) is incomplete. The reverse mapping (filename -> name) is needed at every scan site, and those sites need to know which tool they're scanning for to select the correct stripping rule. Without this, the helper will be architecturally insufficient and the implementer will end up scattering Copilot-specific logic anyway.

**Suggested action**: Design the naming helper as a bidirectional pair:
1. AgentArtifactName(tool, name) -> filename (forward: "reviewer" -> "reviewer.agent.md" for Copilot)
2. AgentResourceName(tool, filename) -> name (reverse: "reviewer.agent.md" -> "reviewer" for Copilot)

Ensure findOrphanedResources and verifyDirectory either receive the tool as a parameter or can infer it from the directory path (already feasible since they know they're scanning a specific tool's agents directory). Update the checklist to require passing tool context to these functions.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Modifications generator (getModificationFilePath, GetModificationPath) hardcodes name+".md" for agents

**What**: In pkg/modifications/generator.go:
- getModificationFilePath (line 213): returns .modifications/<tool>/agents/<name>.md
- GetModificationPath (line 385): checks .modifications/<tool>/agents/<name>.md

The checklist item 4 says to "apply naming helper to generated modifications" and "Copilot agent modifications should be generated with the .agent.md suffix." But getSymlinkSource (installer.go:176) calls GetModificationPath with the tool name and if a modification exists, uses that path as the symlink target.

**Why**: If Copilot modifications are stored as .modifications/copilot/agents/reviewer.agent.md but GetModificationPath still looks for .modifications/copilot/agents/reviewer.md, the modification will never be found. Conversely, if GetModificationPath is updated to look for .agent.md for Copilot, the modification file must also be generated with that name. Both the write path (getModificationFilePath) and the read path (GetModificationPath) must agree, and they must both use the naming helper.

The current GenerateAll (line 280) also filters by filepath.Ext(entry.Name()) != ".md" when scanning the agents directory in the repo. Since repo agents remain .md files per the design, this is fine. But this is a place to add a comment to prevent future confusion.

**Suggested action**: Checklist item 4 should explicitly state that both getModificationFilePath AND GetModificationPath must use the naming helper. The CleanupForResource path (line 326) also constructs the modification path and must agree. Enumerate all three as affected in the instructions.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Minor: Checklist references "repair flows" but doesn't identify cmd/repair.go

**What**: The instructions say "Ensure list/uninstall/repair paths use the same naming rule" but the "Files to Modify" section doesn't list `cmd/repair.go`. The repair flow lives in `cmd/repair.go` (which delegates to `install.Installer` methods) and `cmd/project_verify.go` (which owns `fixVerifyIssues` and `parseResourceFromIssue`). There is no `pkg/install/repair.go`.

**Why**: An implementer reading the checklist will not know where repair code actually lives. The project_verify.go file is the critical one (already flagged in comment #79), but cmd/repair.go should also be scanned for any hardcoded `.md` assumptions in `applyRepairFixes`, `applyRepairBrokenOrWrongRepo`, or `findUnmanagedFiles`.

**Suggested action**: Add `cmd/repair.go` to the "Files to Modify" section as a scan target. Most repair logic delegates to installer methods (which are already being updated), but the repair command itself should be audited for any direct filename construction.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Semver choice v3.3.0 is correct

**What**: Verified all 7 commits in `v3.2.0..HEAD`. The commit `feat(copilot): support GitHub Copilot agents` is a `feat:` commit, which per `docs/contributor-guide/release-process.md` rules triggers a minor bump. No commit bodies contain `BREAKING CHANGE:`. The remaining commits are `fix:`, `refactor:`, `docs:`, and `chore:` — none of which would override a minor bump.

**Why**: Confirming the version choice is the highest-risk item in the plan — a wrong semver causes permanent tag pollution. This one is right.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Acceptance review references wrong archive filename pattern

**What**: Task .6 (Acceptance Review) instruction #3 says to download `aimgr_v3.3.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz`. But GoReleaser's `name_template` in `.goreleaser.yaml` is `{{ .ProjectName }}_{{ .Version }}_{{ .Os }}_{{ .Arch }}` — `{{ .Version }}` strips the `v` prefix. Actual v3.2.0 assets confirm this: the real filename was `aimgr_3.2.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz` (no `v`).

**Why**: A tasker following this instruction literally will try to download a non-existent file and waste time debugging, or worse, conclude the release is broken and file a false-positive bug.

**Suggested action**: Change the example archive name in task .6 instruction #3 from `aimgr_v3.3.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz` to `aimgr_3.3.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz`.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Missing CHANGELOG link reference at bottom of CHANGELOG.md

**What**: Task .2 (Documentation Check) tells the tasker to add a `## [3.3.0] - YYYY-MM-DD` section to CHANGELOG.md but does not mention adding the corresponding link reference at the bottom of the file. The existing CHANGELOG.md has a footer section with link references like `[3.2.0]: https://github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/compare/v3.1.0...v3.2.0`. A new `[3.3.0]` entry needs a matching link reference.

**Why**: Without the link reference, the `[3.3.0]` header renders as plain text instead of a clickable link in GitHub's markdown. Every prior release entry has this reference. A tasker unfamiliar with the CHANGELOG format may not notice.

**Suggested action**: Add an instruction to task .2: "Add the link reference `[3.3.0]: https://github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/compare/v3.2.0...v3.3.0` at the bottom of CHANGELOG.md, following the existing pattern."

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Documentation task .2 should mention the Makefile change from PR #6

**What**: Task .2 instructions say to "Focus on GitHub Copilot agent support and the repair JSON output fix." But the commit range also includes merge PR #6 ("make makefile operating system aware") — commit `adca64d`. This is a contributor-visible change (cross-platform build behavior) that belongs in the changelog under Changed or Fixed.

**Why**: If the tasker follows the instructions literally, they will omit a user/contributor-visible change from the CHANGELOG. The `chore:` and `refactor:` commits are correctly omitted, but a Makefile OS-awareness change affects anyone running `make build`/`make test` and should be noted.

**Suggested action**: Add to task .2 instructions: "Also note the Makefile OS-awareness improvement (PR #6) under a Changed section if it affects build behavior on non-Linux platforms."

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Acceptance review should verify checksums.txt content, not just existence

**What**: Task .6 acceptance criteria say "Release assets include the expected platform archives and checksums.txt." This only checks that checksums.txt exists as an asset. It does not verify that the checksums actually match a downloaded binary.

**Why**: A corrupted or truncated checksums.txt would pass this check. Verifying the checksum of the downloaded binary against checksums.txt is the actual security gate — it confirms GoReleaser produced consistent artifacts.

**Suggested action**: Add an instruction to task .6: "After downloading the archive, also download checksums.txt and verify the archive hash matches: `sha256sum -c checksums.txt --ignore-missing` (or equivalent)."

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Task .3 should pull before pushing to avoid rejected push

**What**: Task .3 instruction #4 says "Push the commit to origin/main" but does not include a `git pull --rebase` before pushing. The AGENTS.md "Landing the Plane" section explicitly mandates `git pull --rebase` before `git push`.

**Why**: If origin/main has advanced since the tasker started (e.g., another agent pushed beads sync commits), `git push` will be rejected. This is especially likely in this project where beads auto-sync can create commits on main. A rejected push mid-release is disruptive and confusing.

**Suggested action**: Add `git pull --rebase` before `git push origin main` in task .3 instruction #4. This matches the project's documented mandatory workflow in AGENTS.md.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Task .4 should include rollback instructions for a failed release workflow

**What**: Task .4 instruction #5 says "Watch the matching release run until completion and stop immediately if it fails." But it gives no guidance on what to do after stopping. The project's `docs/contributor-guide/release-process.md` has a Rollback section with specific commands (`gh release delete`, `git tag -d`, `git push origin :refs/tags/`).

**Why**: If the release workflow fails, a pushed tag `v3.3.0` and possibly a partial/draft GitHub release already exist. A tasker who "stops immediately" without cleaning up leaves the repository in a broken state — the tag is consumed, and re-running requires manual cleanup. The rollback procedure exists in docs but isn't linked from the task.

**Suggested action**: Add to task .4: "If the release workflow fails: (1) delete the GitHub release with `gh release delete v3.3.0 -y`, (2) delete the tag locally and remotely with `git tag -d v3.3.0 && git push origin :refs/tags/v3.3.0`, (3) file a bug for the failure. See docs/contributor-guide/release-process.md § Rollback."

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:

**What**: Verified the dependency chain: .1 (quality gates) ← .2 (docs) ← .3 (commit) ← .4 (tag/release) ← .5 (release notes) ← .6 (acceptance) ← .7 (cleanup). Each task blocks only its immediate successor, and `bd ready` correctly surfaces .1 as the first ready task. The parent-child relationship to the epic is also correct.

**Why**: A broken dependency chain would allow tasks to execute out of order, potentially tagging before the changelog is committed or writing release notes before the release exists. This chain is sound.

**Suggested action**: None. Dependencies are correctly modeled.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## No missing critical release steps

**What**: Cross-referenced the plan against `docs/RELEASING.md`, `docs/contributor-guide/release-process.md`, `.github/workflows/release.yml`, and `.goreleaser.yaml`. All documented release steps are represented: quality gates → CHANGELOG update → commit/push → annotated tag → workflow monitoring → release notes → verification → cleanup. The plan correctly identifies that version files must NOT be edited (tag-only versioning via GoReleaser ldflags). The plan correctly uses `git tag -a` (annotated) rather than lightweight tags.

**Why**: A missing step in a release plan is the most dangerous failure mode — it only surfaces during execution when recovery is harder.

**Suggested action**: None. No critical steps are missing.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## --prune-package fate is unresolved — this blocks implementation

**What**: Instruction #5 says "keep or adapt `--prune-package` only if [it] still make[s] sense under reconciliation semantics" but makes no decision. The current `--prune-package` implementation in `repair.go` has a complex interactive escalation flow (`resolveInvalidRefs()` with sync/repair/remove/skip options) spanning ~200 lines. A tasker implementing this will either (a) guess wrong and need rework, or (b) block waiting for clarification.

**Why**: Under reconciliation semantics, repair already removes undeclared content and reinstalls declared content — which is most of what `--prune-package` does today. But `--prune-package` also edits `ai.package.yaml` (removes invalid refs) and offers repo-level recovery (sync/repair), which reconciliation does NOT do. These are fundamentally different operations. Leaving this undecided means the tasker must make an architectural call that belongs in planning.

**Suggested action**: Make an explicit decision in the task description:
- Option A: Remove `--prune-package` entirely — reconciliation subsumes the filesystem side; manifest editing becomes a separate concern
- Option B: Keep `--prune-package` as "validate and clean ai.package.yaml entries against repo" — orthogonal to reconciliation
- Option C: Fold the repo-level recovery (sync/repair) into the reconciliation flow as automatic fallback

I'd recommend Option A for simplicity, with a follow-up task for manifest validation if needed.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## --dry-run behavior undefined for reconciliation — safety regression risk

**What**: Instruction #5 says "keep or adapt `--dry-run` ... only if [it] still make[s] sense under reconciliation semantics" without specifying what dry-run output looks like for the new reconciliation flow. The current dry-run only covers `--reset` and `--prune-package` paths — there's no dry-run for the core repair path today.

**Why**: Reconciliation is now destructive by default (removes undeclared content). Without dry-run, users have no way to preview what repair will delete before it does. This is a significant safety regression from the current behavior where destructive actions require explicit `--reset` or `--force`. A tasker needs to know exactly what dry-run should report: planned deletions, planned installs, planned fixes — and whether these appear in structured output.

**Suggested action**: Make `--dry-run` a required feature (not optional) in the acceptance criteria. Add a criterion: "`--dry-run` reports all planned deletions, installs, and fixes without making filesystem changes, in both table and JSON format." This is not a nice-to-have — it's a safety requirement given that reconciliation is destructive.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Reconciliation ordering (delete-first vs install-first) is unspecified

**What**: The instructions say "remove extra files" (step 3) then "reinstall or fix every desired resource" (step 4), implying delete-first. But this isn't explicitly called out as a design decision, and partial failure recovery depends entirely on this ordering.

**Why**: If repair deletes first and then crashes mid-install, the user is left with fewer resources than before AND fewer than the manifest declares — a strictly worse state. If repair installs first and then deletes, a crash leaves extra content but all declared resources are present — a recoverable state. The ordering also affects behavior when a declared resource already exists but is wrong (e.g., broken symlink): does repair delete it as "extra" and then reinstall, or does it fix in-place?

**Suggested action**: Add an explicit instruction: "Install/fix declared resources first, then delete undeclared content. This ensures partial failures leave the project in a recoverable state." Alternatively, if delete-first is intended, document that `aimgr repair` is idempotent and safe to re-run after partial failure.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## RepairResult JSON schema is a breaking change — not called out

**What**: The current `RepairResult` struct returns `fixed`, `failed`, `hints`, and `summary` fields. Under reconciliation, the output must include removals (undeclared content deleted) and installs/fixes separately. This is a breaking change to the JSON output schema that scripts and automation may depend on.

**Why**: CODING.md and the project conventions emphasize structured output for scripting. Changing the JSON schema without acknowledging it as a breaking change means downstream consumers (CI scripts, the AI resource manager skill, automation) will silently receive unexpected data. The acceptance criteria say "Table and JSON output plus automated tests reflect the new behavior" but don't flag this as a breaking change requiring migration guidance.

**Suggested action**: Add to acceptance criteria: "JSON output schema changes are documented as breaking changes." Consider whether a deprecation period or version bump is appropriate. At minimum, the docs task (k209) should include output-formats.md updates with before/after examples.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Test breakage scope is larger than "Files to Modify" suggests

**What**: The "Files to Modify" section lists `cmd/repair_test.go` and `test/repair_test.go`. But `cmd/project_verify_test.go` also has repair-related assertions (orphan detection, fix suggestions referencing `aimgr repair`), and `cmd/repair_test.go` line 39 has `TestRepair_CommandFlagsExist` that explicitly checks for `["format", "reset", "prune-package", "force", "dry-run", "project-path"]` — this will fail immediately when flags are removed.

**Why**: If the tasker only modifies the listed files, the build will break on tests in files they weren't told to touch. This causes confusion and rework. The verify task (z2en) covers `project_verify_test.go` but it depends on bhiq — so bhiq could merge and break CI before z2en starts.

**Suggested action**: Either (a) add `cmd/project_verify_test.go` to bhiq's Files to Modify with a note to at minimum fix the flag-check assertions that will break, or (b) acknowledge in bhiq that CI may be temporarily red until z2en completes, and ensure z2en is sequenced immediately after.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Clean has no structured output (--format/JSON) — scripting gap

**What**: The current `clean.go` has no `--format` flag and returns only `(removed, failed)` counts. The plan doesn't add structured output. But the acceptance review (2kkq) says "Verify output ... match the implemented behavior" — and any automation consuming clean results needs to know what was deleted.

**Why**: Per CODING.md conventions and the project's existing pattern (repair, verify, list all have `--format json`), clean is the odd command out without structured output. If clean now wipes entire directories unconditionally, operators need to know what was removed — especially in CI/CD pipelines. Adding it later means another breaking change.

**Suggested action**: Add `--format` and JSON output to zqso's acceptance criteria. The output should list each removed entry with path and type (file/symlink/directory). This is one of those "easier to add now than retrofit" situations.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## No safety gate on clean — asymmetric with repair

**What**: The epic specifies that `aimgr repair` must fail safely when `ai.package.yaml` is missing or invalid. But `aimgr clean` has no such gate — it will wipe owned directories regardless of manifest state. This is intentional per the design ("clean empties owned resource directories"), but the asymmetry creates a footgun.

**Why**: Consider: user accidentally runs `aimgr clean` in a directory without `ai.package.yaml`. Clean wipes everything. Then `aimgr repair` fails because there's no manifest. The user has now lost all installed resources with no automatic recovery path. The old `--yes` confirmation at least gave a chance to abort. Under the new model, the `clean → repair` workflow only works if a valid manifest exists, but clean doesn't check.

**Suggested action**: Either (a) add a warning (not a blocker) when clean runs and no `ai.package.yaml` exists: "Warning: no manifest found — `aimgr repair` will not be able to restore resources", or (b) explicitly document in the task that this footgun is accepted and the docs task (k209) should call it out. I'd lean toward (a) — it's one `if` statement and prevents support requests.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Behavior when no tool directories exist is unspecified

**What**: Current `clean.go` returns early with a "no tool directories found" message when there are no tool dirs. The plan's instructions don't mention this edge case, and the acceptance criteria don't cover it.

**Why**: Minor, but a tasker might change this behavior inadvertently during the rewrite. The current early-return is correct behavior — clean should be a no-op when there's nothing to clean. Without specifying it, the acceptance review has nothing to verify against.

**Suggested action**: Add to acceptance criteria: "`aimgr clean` is a no-op with informative message when no tool directories are detected."

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Verify's deprecated --fix flag not addressed

**What**: `project_verify.go` has a deprecated `--fix` flag that prints "Deprecated: use aimgr repair instead." The plan's instructions say "Ensure verify guidance never references removed flags" but don't mention removing or updating `--fix` itself. Under the new model, `--fix` should either be removed entirely or its deprecation message should reference the new repair semantics.

**Why**: If `--fix` remains with its current message, it's technically still accurate ("use aimgr repair") but misses the opportunity to tell users about the new reconciliation behavior. More importantly, if the verify task is supposed to be the alignment task, leaving a deprecated flag without addressing it is an incomplete alignment.

**Suggested action**: Add to instructions: "Remove the deprecated `--fix` flag entirely, or update its deprecation message to describe the new repair reconciliation behavior." Given that this is a breaking-change release anyway, removing it cleanly is the simpler choice.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Issue type reclassification needs explicit mapping

**What**: Current verify uses issue types: `broken`, `wrong-repo`, `not-installed`, `orphaned`, `unreadable`. Under the owned-folder model, "orphaned" no longer makes sense — everything undeclared is simply "extra content" to be removed. The instructions say "Replace or adapt issue classifications" but don't provide a target mapping.

**Why**: The issue types appear in JSON output and may be consumed by scripts, the AI resource manager skill, or CI automation. Changing type names without a clear mapping means the tasker invents names, which may not match what bhiq's repair output expects. verify and repair must use consistent terminology.

**Suggested action**: Define the target issue type mapping in the instructions. Suggested: `broken` → keep, `wrong-repo` → keep or merge into `broken`, `not-installed` → keep (declared but missing), `orphaned` → rename to `undeclared` or `extra`, `unreadable` → keep. This ensures verify and repair speak the same language.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Missing files: SKILL.md content not verified, concepts.md listed but unchecked

**What**: The Files to Modify list includes `ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/SKILL.md` and `docs/user-guide/concepts.md`, but neither was analyzed during planning to determine what changes are needed. The SKILL.md may contain repair/clean workflow examples that reference removed flags — and `concepts.md` likely defines the managed/unmanaged/orphan mental model that the epic explicitly removes.

**Why**: If the docs tasker discovers that `concepts.md` has deep references to the old model (which it almost certainly does, given the epic's goal is to remove that mental model), the scope of the docs task grows significantly. The tasker needs to know upfront whether this is a light edit or a conceptual rewrite.

**Suggested action**: Read `docs/user-guide/concepts.md` and `ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/SKILL.md` during planning (or add a note to the task: "Audit these files first to determine scope before starting edits"). Also verify whether `CONTRIBUTING.md` or `AGENTS.md` reference repair/clean workflows that need updating — these aren't in the file list.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Migration guidance for users of removed flags is not in acceptance criteria

**What**: Instruction #4 says "Add upgrade notes for the breaking CLI behavior changes where appropriate." But the acceptance criteria only check that "Removed flags are absent from active examples" and "Breaking behavior changes are called out in release-facing docs or changelog entries." There's no criterion for actually explaining the migration path.

**Why**: Users running `aimgr repair --reset --force` in scripts will get errors after upgrading. They need to know: (1) `--reset` + `--force` is now the default behavior of `repair`, (2) `clean` + `repair` is the new equivalent of the old `--reset` flow, (3) `--yes` removal from clean means scripts don't need to change (no prompt means no flag needed). Without this, users file bug reports or downgrade.

**Suggested action**: Add acceptance criterion: "A migration/upgrade section exists in repair.md or CHANGELOG.md that maps old flag usage to new command equivalents." Specifically: `repair --reset` → `repair`, `repair --force` → `repair`, `clean --yes` → `clean`, `repair --reset --force` → `clean && repair`.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Acceptance criteria are too vague for a verifier to pass/fail

**What**: The four criteria are high-level summaries of the epic goals, not testable assertions. For example: "Verify output, command help, and docs match the implemented behavior" — match how? A verifier needs specific checks.

**Why**: The acceptance review is the quality gate before the epic closes. If the verifier has to guess what "match the implemented behavior" means, they'll either rubber-stamp it or spend time re-deriving the requirements. Both outcomes defeat the purpose of an acceptance review.

**Suggested action**: Expand criteria into specific verifiable checks. Example:
- `aimgr repair --help` does NOT show `--reset` or `--force`
- `aimgr clean --help` does NOT show `--yes`
- `aimgr repair` with missing `ai.package.yaml` exits non-zero with error message
- `aimgr repair --dry-run` lists planned changes without modifying filesystem
- `aimgr clean` removes all content from commands/skills/agents dirs (verify with `ls`)
- `aimgr verify` output does not contain "orphaned", "unmanaged", or references to removed flags
- `aimgr repair --format json` output schema includes removals and installs
- `go test ./...` passes
- `golangci-lint run` passes
- Grep for `--reset`, `--force`, `--yes`, `orphan`, `unmanaged` in docs returns zero hits (outside changelogs)

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## clean→repair is the new --reset, but this migration path is undocumented in the plan

**What**: The old workflow was `aimgr repair --reset --force` (nuke and reinstall). The new equivalent is `aimgr clean && aimgr repair` (wipe owned dirs, then reconcile from manifest). This is the most important user-facing migration, but it's never stated explicitly in any task or the epic description.

**Why**: Without documenting this equivalence: (1) the docs task (k209) won't know to write migration guidance for it, (2) the acceptance review (2kkq) won't verify it works end-to-end, (3) users won't discover it. The whole point of removing `--reset` is that the two-command workflow is cleaner — but if nobody writes it down, users just see that `--reset` disappeared.

**Suggested action**: Add to the epic description under Decisions or Goals: "The `aimgr clean && aimgr repair` workflow replaces the old `aimgr repair --reset --force` behavior." This ensures all downstream tasks (docs, acceptance) inherit the context.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Simplification opportunity: bhiq and zqso can execute in parallel

**What**: The dependency graph shows bhiq (repair) and zqso (clean) both block z2en, k209, and 2kkq — but they don't depend on each other. This is correct for parallel execution. However, the task descriptions don't acknowledge shared concerns: both tasks touch the "owned resource directories" concept, both need to agree on which directories are "owned," and both should use the same directory-listing helper.

**Why**: If two taskers work on bhiq and zqso independently, they might implement different logic for "which directories are owned." Today, `clean.go` uses `tools.DetectToolsConfig()` while `repair.go` uses its own tool detection. The reconciliation model needs these to be consistent. A shared helper prevents divergence and reduces total code.

**Suggested action**: Add a coordination note to both bhiq and zqso: "The list of owned resource directories must be consistent with the other task. Extract a shared helper (e.g., `getOwnedResourceDirs()`) if one doesn't exist, or reuse the existing detection logic from the same entry point." This prevents integration issues when z2en tries to align verify with both.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## aimgr uninstall overlap not addressed in out-of-scope

**What**: The epic's out-of-scope says "Unrelated changes to package/repository management beyond what is needed to keep `repair` coherent." But `aimgr uninstall` removes individual resources from owned directories — which is exactly what `aimgr repair` now also does (removes undeclared content). The behavioral overlap between "uninstall resource X" and "repair removes X because it's undeclared" isn't addressed.

**Why**: If a user runs `aimgr uninstall foo-skill` but doesn't update `ai.package.yaml`, then runs `aimgr repair`, repair will reinstall `foo-skill` because it's still declared. This is probably correct behavior (repair reconciles to manifest), but it's a behavior change from today where uninstall and repair are independent. Users may be surprised.

**Suggested action**: This doesn't need a code change, but it needs documentation. Add a note to the docs task (k209) or the epic: "Document that `aimgr repair` will reinstall any declared resources that were manually uninstalled. To permanently remove a resource, remove it from `ai.package.yaml` first." This is the natural consequence of the owned-folder model and should be called out explicitly.

author: Hans Kohlreiter
text:
## Plan Review: Overall Assessment — YELLOW (address before execution)

### Rating: YELLOW — Good design direction, but 3 blocking ambiguities must be resolved before taskers start

The owned-folder model is a genuine simplification. The epic breakdown is logical, the dependency graph is correct, and the parallel execution of bhiq/zqso is well-structured. However, several design decisions are left to taskers that should be made in planning.

### Top 3 Risks (blocking)

1. **`--prune-package` is undecided** (bhiq): ~200 lines of complex interactive code with no clear disposition. A tasker will either remove valuable functionality or preserve dead code. This must be decided before implementation.

2. **`--dry-run` is optional but should be mandatory** (bhiq): Reconciliation is destructive by default. Without dry-run, there's no preview mechanism. This is a safety regression from the current model where destructive actions require explicit flags.

3. **Reconciliation ordering unspecified** (bhiq): Delete-first vs install-first determines partial failure behavior. This is an architectural decision, not an implementation detail.

### Top 3 Risks (non-blocking but important)

4. **Clean has no safety warning when manifest is missing** (zqso): `clean` wipes everything; `repair` then fails because no manifest. One warning message prevents this footgun.

5. **JSON output schema breaking change not acknowledged** (bhiq): Scripts consuming `RepairResult` will break silently.

6. **Acceptance criteria too vague to verify** (2kkq): A verifier can't pass/fail against "match the implemented behavior."

### Plan Gaps Requiring Action

- Add explicit `--prune-package` decision to bhiq
- Make `--dry-run` a required acceptance criterion on bhiq
- Specify reconciliation ordering in bhiq instructions
- Add `--format`/JSON output to zqso acceptance criteria
- Define issue type mapping (orphaned → ?) in z2en
- Expand 2kkq acceptance criteria into specific testable checks
- Document `clean && repair` as the `--reset` migration path in epic description
- Add uninstall/repair interaction note to k209
- Verify `concepts.md` and `SKILL.md` content before starting k209

### Required Test Matrix (for implementation and acceptance)

**Repair (bhiq):**
- Reconcile: manifest with 3 resources, 1 extra file → extra removed, 3 installed
- Reconcile: manifest with package ref → expanded, all members installed
- Reconcile: broken symlink for declared resource → fixed
- Reconcile: empty owned dirs → all manifest resources installed
- Safety: missing ai.package.yaml → error, no filesystem changes
- Safety: invalid ai.package.yaml → error, no filesystem changes
- Dry-run: shows planned deletions/installs, no changes made
- JSON output: new schema with removals and installs arrays
- Partial failure: install fails mid-way → already-installed resources remain
- No manifest resources → owned dirs cleaned (or error?)

**Clean (zqso):**
- Wipe: symlinks, files, directories, nested content all removed
- Wipe: broken symlinks removed (not skipped like today)
- Preserve: ai.package.yaml untouched
- Preserve: non-resource tool config files untouched
- No tool dirs: informative message, exit 0
- Format: JSON output lists removed entries (if added per recommendation)

**Verify (z2en):**
- Extra content detected and labeled correctly (not "orphaned")
- Repair suggestions reference new semantics
- No output contains "orphaned", "unmanaged", "managed"
- No output references --reset, --force, --yes
- JSON output schema consistent with repair's new schema

### Documentation Checklist (k209)

- [ ] `docs/user-guide/repair.md` — rewrite for reconciliation, add migration table
- [ ] `docs/user-guide/getting-started.md` — update examples
- [ ] `docs/user-guide/README.md` — remove --reset/unmanaged references
- [ ] `docs/user-guide/concepts.md` — rewrite owned-folder model (likely significant)
- [ ] `docs/reference/troubleshooting.md` — replace --reset troubleshooting
- [ ] `docs/reference/output-formats.md` — update JSON schema examples
- [ ] `ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/SKILL.md` — audit and update
- [ ] `ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/references/install-uninstall.md` — remove --reset/--force/--yes
- [ ] `CHANGELOG.md` — breaking changes section with migration guidance
- [ ] Verify `CONTRIBUTING.md` and `AGENTS.md` don't reference removed concepts

### Execution Readiness: NOT YET — resolve the 3 blocking ambiguities first

The plan is structurally sound but has holes that will cause rework if taskers start now. Resolve `--prune-package`, `--dry-run`, and reconciliation ordering decisions on bhiq, then this is ready to execute.
